elements.sent-2411-387: Existing evaluation frameworks largely focus on the model level~\cite{xia2024ai , assessing LLM capabilities in areas such as coding~\cite{chen2021evaluating,zhuo2024bigcodebench,du2023classeval and domain-specific applications (e.g., healthcare~\cite{liu2024benchmarking,cai2024medbench , legal~\cite{fei2023lawbench,guha2024legalbench , and finance~\cite{xie2023pixiu,islam2023financebench ).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-388: While informative, these benchmarks often fail to account for system-level sociotechnical interactions in complex, multi-component agents~\cite{liuagentbench,gioacchini2024agentquest .}
elements.sent-2411-389: Many evaluation methods for LLM agents rely heavily on fixed benchmarks, which pose significant limitations.
elements.sent-2411-390: These static tests risk data contamination~\cite{sainz2023nlp and fail to adapt to the dynamic, evolving contexts of real-world applications.}
elements.sent-2411-391: While live benchmarks (e.g.,~\cite{white2024livebench, jain2024livecodebench ) have emerged as a promising alternative, offering improved adaptability through periodic updates, they still fall short of addressing the core challenges of LLM agent evaluation.}
elements.sent-2411-392: Specifically, these benchmarks are not truly "live" in the sense of enabling in-situ, real-time assessment within interactive environments.
elements.sent-2411-393: As a result, both fixed and periodically updated benchmarks struggle to adequately capture the complexities and emergent behaviors of LLM agents in practical, real-time settings.
elements.sent-2411-394: System-level evaluation frameworks have garnered significant attention, with many approaches emphasizing end-to-end success metrics (e.g., success/pass rate) as the primary measure of performance.
elements.sent-2411-395: While these metrics provide a holistic view of system effectiveness, they often overlook critical intermediate decision-making steps, which are essential for diagnosing failures and understanding system behavior~\cite{gioacchini2024agentquest .}
elements.sent-2411-396: To address this limitation, step-based and trajectory-based evaluation methods have been proposed~\cite{gioacchini2024agentquest, xiong2024watch .}
elements.sent-2411-397: These methods offer finer granularity, enabling detailed insights into workflow efficiency, agent reasoning, and the progression of decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-398: However, while such granular approaches enhance diagnostic capabilities, they also introduce trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-399: Overemphasis on step-level or trajectory-level analysis can lead to excessive complexity, making it challenging to aggregate results into a coherent system-level assessment \cite{bentonsabotage .}
elements.sent-2411-400: Therefore, a careful balance must be struck between granularity and holistic evaluation to ensure that frameworks are both diagnostically informative and practically actionable.
elements.sent-2411-401: Another emerging approach is the use of safety cases, which provide structured, evidence-based justifications for LLM agent reliability~\cite{buhl2024safety,aisi2024safetycases .}
elements.sent-2411-402: These frameworks help regulators and developers assess operational risks but require continuous updates to remain relevant in evolving deployment conditions.
elements.sent-2411-403: Despite these advancements, current methods lack a structured approach that integrates evaluation results into continuous system improvement and real-time risk control—a gap that evaluation-driven development aims to address.
elements.sent-2411-404: This study employs a MLR\footnote{The research protocol and data extraction form are uploaded as supplementary materials for review purpose.}
elements.sent-2411-405: MLR was chosen because it allows for a comprehensive understanding of both academic theories and industry practices.
elements.sent-2411-406: The findings from the MLR directly informed the process model and reference architecture, ensuring they are empirically grounded and aligned with real-world evaluation challenges.
elements.sent-2411-407: The planning phase began with identifying the need for a multivocal approach to capture both research-driven and practitioner-driven perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-408: An initial research protocol was developed, including explicit RQs, search terms, and inclusion/exclusion criteria to ensure comprehensive coverage.
elements.sent-2411-409: A pilot study was conducted to test the effectiveness and relevance of search queries, leading to refinements in the final protocol.
elements.sent-2411-410: Databases and Search Strategy: Academic sources were retrieved from Google Scholar, IEEE Xplore, ACM Digital Library, Science Direct, and Springer, while Google Search was used to identify relevant grey literature (e.g., practitioner blogs, evaluation frameworks, open-source tools).
elements.sent-2411-411: The primary search terms were: ("large language model" OR "LLM" OR "agent") AND ("evaluate" OR "benchmark" OR "test"), with variations (e.g., pluralization, noun-verb shifts, and verb forms) to maximize retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-412: Searches were performed on June 5, 2024, with a focus on literature published since 2022, reflecting post-ChatGPT advancements.
elements.sent-2411-413: Screening and Selection: After removing duplicates, sources were screened in three stages: title, abstract, and full-text review.
elements.sent-2411-414: Two authors independently conducted each stage to ensure consistency.
elements.sent-2411-415: Inter-rater reliability was calculated using Cohen’s Kappa (\( \kappa = 0.78 \)), indicating strong agreement.
elements.sent-2411-417: The selection criteria prioritized sources addressing tools, frameworks, or platforms for LLM agent evaluation with clear theoretical or empirical contributions.
elements.sent-2411-418: Excluded sources included those unrelated to evaluation, lacking credibility or substantial contributions, or inadequately documented tools.
elements.sent-2411-419: Eligible sources included academic papers, conference proceedings, technical reports, white papers, and preprints (e.g., arXiv) available in English.
elements.sent-2411-420: Quality Assessment: To ensure rigor, academic sources were evaluated using three criteria:.
elements.sent-2411-421: Authority: Assessed based on venue reputation and author credentials.
elements.sent-2411-422: Methodological Rigor: Evaluated for explicit methodology, reproducibility, and depth of analysis.
elements.sent-2411-423: Objectivity: Ensured neutrality and avoidance of promotional bias.
elements.sent-2411-424: For grey literature and tools, credibility was assessed based on:.
elements.sent-2411-425: Source Reputation: Evaluated based on publisher standing (e.g., company reports, reputable blogs) or tool popularity (e.g., GitHub stars).
elements.sent-2411-426: Documentation: Presence of clear usage guides, benchmarks, or test cases.
elements.sent-2411-427: Maintenance Activity: Frequency of updates and community engagement.
elements.sent-2411-428: Finally, our analysis included 134 academic sources and 27 tools, frameworks, and platforms for LLM agent evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-429: Data Extraction and Mapping: Data was extracted using a structured coding framework, mapping key evaluation dimensions relevant to the research questions.. This included:.
elements.sent-2411-430: Evaluation Practices: Coverage across pre-deployment, post-deployment, and continuous monitoring.
elements.sent-2411-431: Evaluation Methods: Types of metrics used (e.g., end-to-end vs. intermediate pipeline/artifact assessments).
elements.sent-2411-432: Evaluation Outputs and Adaptation Mechanisms: How results inform system refinement, risk control, and iterative improvements.
elements.sent-2411-433: Architectural Considerations: Evaluation-related components in system design, including feedback loops, observability infrastructure, and safety mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-434: These insights directly shaped the process model (capturing procedural evaluation integration) and the reference architecture (capturing architectural evaluation integration).
elements.sent-2411-435: Data Synthesis: A thematic analysis identified recurring evaluation patterns, challenges, and best practices.
elements.sent-2411-436: To complement qualitative findings, quantitative frequency counts were computed to assess the distribution of evaluation methods, system coverage, and adaptation strategies.
elements.sent-2411-437: Findings were synthesized to guide the process model and reference architecture, ensuring that both are: (1) Empirically grounded, (2) Aligned with practical challenges, and (3) Facilitative of real-world agent evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-438: The resulting reference architecture was designed to be industry-cross-cutting, conceptually rigorous, and facilitative, aligning with established principles of empirically grounded reference architectures~\cite{galster2011empirically .}
elements.sent-2411-439: To enhance robustness, the synthesized themes underwent triangulation through internal review and were further validated via feedback from co-authors specializing in LLM agent design and evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-440: This iterative validation process ensured that the findings were not only methodologically sound but also practically applicable to real-world AI system evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-441: To address RQ1, the MLR, comprising 134 academic and 27 grey sources, reveals several critical challenges in current evaluation practices that impede LLM agent design.
elements.sent-2411-442: A key limitation in LLM agent evaluation is the overwhelming pre-deployment focus, with limited attention to post-deployment and continuous evaluation (see Fig. \ref{fig:dis-lifecycle ).}
elements.sent-2411-443: Our analysis shows that in academic literature, 93.28\% (125/134) of evaluations occur before deployment, while post-deployment (2.24\%, 3/134) and continuous evaluation (4.48\%, 6/134) remain severely underexplored.
elements.sent-2411-444: While offline evaluations help validate performance under controlled conditions, they fail to capture emergent behaviors, operational degradation, and real-world interactions that arise post-deployment.
elements.figure-2411-006: \includegraphics[width=0.6\linewidth]{evaluation_stages.pdf} \caption{Distribution of Evaluation Efforts Across Lifecycle Stages} \label{fig:dis-lifecycle}
elements.sent-2411-445: By contrast, grey literature presents a more balanced distribution: 44.44\% (12/27) of evaluations occur pre-deployment, 14.81\% (4/27) post-deployment, and 40.74\% (11/27) continuously.
elements.sent-2411-446: This suggests that industry-driven practices recognize the need for ongoing monitoring, though post-deployment evaluation remains underrepresented even in grey sources, indicating that real-world observability mechanisms are still insufficiently integrated.
elements.sent-2411-447: Implications: The lack of lifecycle-spanning evaluation mirrors the technical debt phenomenon in traditional software engineering~\cite{cunningham1992wycash , where early-stage testing limitations incur operational costs.}
elements.sent-2411-448: In LLM agents, the consequences include:.
elements.sent-2411-449: Missed Real-World Failures: Pre-deployment tests cannot detect emergent issues (e.g., adversarial attacks, data drift, tool misuse) that manifest only in production.
elements.sent-2411-450: Reactive Mitigation: Without post-deployment monitoring, issues such as bias amplification or security vulnerabilities are only detected after affecting users.
elements.sent-2411-451: Siloed Insights: Disconnected evaluation phases prevent feedback loops where real-world usage data could inform iterative refinements.
elements.sent-2411-452: Addressing this fragmentation requires evaluation frameworks that span the entire lifecycle, aligning with AgentOps principles~\cite{dong2024taxonomy to support ongoing monitoring, adaptation, and refinement.}
elements.sent-2411-453: LLM agent evaluation is often constrained by the dominance of high-level and aggregated metrics, which obscure critical system-level insights.
elements.sent-2411-454: Our analysis (see Fig. \ref{fig:dis-metrics ) reveals that 66.42\% (89/134) of academic studies rely predominantly on aggregate measures such as overall accuracy, task success rate, or binary pass/fail outcomes.}
elements.sent-2411-455: While these metrics offer a broad performance snapshot, they fail to capture fine-grained failure patterns, such as planning errors, tool integration failures, or inconsistencies in multi-step decision-making—all of which are crucial for diagnosing and improving LLM agent behavior.
elements.sent-2411-456: Despite their diagnostic limitations, aggregated metrics remain the dominant evaluation approach in academic studies.
elements.sent-2411-457: Only 21.64\% (29/134) assess intermediate pipelines and artifacts, and an even smaller fraction (11.94\%, 16/134) employ mixed evaluations that integrate both intermediate and end-to-end insights.
elements.sent-2411-458: This imbalance suggests that most academic evaluations focus on outcome-based assessments.
elements.sent-2411-459: By treating evaluation as a final validation step rather than a continuous diagnostic tool, these studies overlook the complexities of real-world agent behavior.
elements.sent-2411-460: In contrast, grey literature places greater emphasis on evaluating system-level interactions.
elements.sent-2411-461: This distribution reflects a stronger industry-driven focus on assessing LLM agents in real-world operational contexts.
elements.sent-2411-462: While intermediate evaluations provide deeper insights into system behavior, they introduce several practical challenges \cite{bentonsabotage .}
elements.sent-2411-463: First, narrowly scoped evaluations often struggle to establish meaningful performance thresholds, as they may not fully capture real-world complexities or cumulative risks.
elements.sent-2411-464: Second, consolidating multiple fine-grained assessments into a comprehensive system-wide evaluation remains difficult, as fragmented evaluations make it unclear how different capabilities contribute to overall performance.
elements.sent-2411-465: Third, these challenges are compounded by the increased cost and complexity of designing and executing detailed assessments, particularly when domain expertise or extensive manual review is required.
elements.sent-2411-466: Thus, while intermediate evaluations offer higher diagnostic value, they must be carefully integrated with end-to-end evaluations to ensure both interpretability and practical scalability.
elements.sent-2411-467: Implications: The over-reliance on aggregated metrics limits the ability to diagnose and improve LLM agent behavior.
elements.sent-2411-468: By focusing only on final outputs, such metrics obscure how agents reason, plan, and interact with external tools, making it difficult to pinpoint the root causes of failures.
elements.sent-2411-469: This lack of process-level visibility prevents targeted refinements, as developers are unable to systematically adjust prompts, retrieval mechanisms, or tool integrations based on evaluation insights.
elements.sent-2411-470: Without deeper assessments, failures in decision-making, reasoning steps, or tool usage may go unnoticed until deployment, leading to unreliable and suboptimal agent performance.
elements.subsubsection-2411-007: Gaps Between Model-Level and System-Level Evaluations
elements.sent-2411-471: Current evaluation disproportionately focus on the underlying models, often at the expense of system-level evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-472: Our analysis (see Fig. \ref{fig:model-vs-system ) reveals that 66.42\% (89/134) of academic studies focus primarily on evaluating model performance—such as response coherence, factual correctness, and task-specific accuracy—while only 21.64\% (29/134) assess system-level interactions.}
elements.sent-2411-473: Even fewer studies (11.94\%, 16/134) employ integrated evaluations that jointly examine model- and system-level dynamics.
elements.sent-2411-474: In contrast, grey literature adopts a more system-aware perspective.
elements.sent-2411-475: This divergence suggests that academic research tends to evaluate LLMs as standalone models, whereas industry-driven evaluations recognize that agents must function within a broader operational ecosystem, interacting with external tools, decision workflows, and user environments.
elements.sent-2411-476: Implications: The disconnect between model-level evaluations and real-world system operations has several critical consequences:.
elements.sent-2411-477: Evaluation outcomes do not reflect real-world agent behavior: Model-level evaluations alone overlook how agents operate within workflows, integrate with external tools, and adapt to dynamic environments.
elements.sent-2411-478: As a result, models that perform well in isolation may fail in deployment due to execution delays, tool failures, or cascading decision errors.
elements.sent-2411-479: Risk of misaligned refinement: Prioritizing model-level evaluations may lead to optimizations that do not translate into real-world effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-480: For example, improving factual correctness does not necessarily enhance an agent’s ability to handle structured tasks requiring API interactions.
elements.sent-2411-481: A fundamental limitation in current LLM agent evaluation is the dominance of predefined, static benchmarks and test cases, which assume fixed conditions and fail to capture the complexity and unpredictability of real-world interactions.
elements.sent-2411-482: Our analysis (see Fig. \ref{fig:adaptive-evals ) reveals that only 2.24\% (3/134) of academic studies explicitly incorporate evaluation methodologies that move beyond static test cases.}
elements.sent-2411-483: In contrast, grey literature shows a somewhat higher emphasis on contextual variability, with 18.52\% (5/27) integrating evaluation components that extend beyond fixed benchmarks.
elements.sent-2411-484: Despite this, the vast majority of evaluations in both academic (97.76\%, 131/134) and grey literature (81.48\%, 22/27) remain grounded in predefined test scenarios, limiting their ability to evaluate an agent in dynamic and evolving settings.
elements.figure-2411-009: \begin{minipage}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{adaptive_vs_static_evaluations.pdf} \caption{Comparison of Adaptive vs. Static Evaluations in Academic vs. Grey Literature} \label{fig:adaptive-evals} \end{minipage} \begin{minipage}{0.48\textwidth} \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{evaluation_as_checkpoints.pdf} \caption{Comparison of Evaluation-Driven Adaptation vs. Evaluation as Checkpoints} \label{fig:evaluation-adaptation} \end{minipage}
elements.sent-2411-485: Implications: Static evaluations present a fundamental methodological limitation: predefined benchmarks inherently fail to capture the full range of conditions and challenges LLM agents encounter post-deployment.
elements.sent-2411-486: This reflects the curse of dimensionality—as the complexity of tasks, input variations, and external interactions increases, static evaluation sets become increasingly inadequate~\cite{burden2024evaluating .}
elements.sent-2411-487: Even large-scale benchmarks cover only a fraction of potential real-world scenarios, leading to evaluations that provide an incomplete and potentially misleading picture of an agent’s robustness and safety.
elements.sent-2411-488: As a result, models optimized for benchmark success may fail to generalize, struggle with unforeseen inputs, or exhibit performance breakdowns when faced with real-world variability.
elements.sent-2411-489: The continued reliance on predefined test cases risks reinforcing superficial improvements rather than advancing truly robust AI capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-490: To ensure evaluation results align with deployment realities, future methodologies must move beyond fixed benchmarks and integrate more contextually adaptive testing approaches that dynamically expose agents to a broader and more representative range of task conditions \cite{US_AISI_2025 .}
elements.subsubsection-2411-009: Failure to Leverage Evaluation for Continuous Agent Improvement
elements.sent-2411-491: Although extensive evaluation data is available, its impact on agent refinement remains limited.
elements.sent-2411-492: Our analysis (see Fig. \ref{fig:evaluation-adaptation ) reveals that only 29.10\% (39/134) of academic studies explicitly report leveraging evaluation results to drive iterative design improvements.}
elements.sent-2411-493: In contrast, 81.48\% (22/27) of grey literature sources incorporate evaluation feedback into the refinement of agent behavior, architectures, or tool integration.
elements.sent-2411-494: This discrepancy suggests that, in academic research, evaluations are often treated as isolated validation steps rather than continuous inputs into the design process.
elements.sent-2411-495: Specifically, in academic sources, 70.90\% (95/134) of studies rely on static evaluation checkpoints, where agent performance and safety is measured at predefined milestones (e.g., final benchmark scores) without mechanisms for systematic adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-496: Grey literature, however, exhibits a more iterative approach, indicating that evaluation-driven refinements are considered a core part of agent development in industry applications.
elements.sent-2411-497: Implications: The lack of evaluation-driven adaptation limits the robustness and responsiveness of LLM agents, leading to several key challenges:.
elements.sent-2411-498: Failure to close the loop between evaluation and development: Treating evaluation as a static checkpoint rather than an iterative process prevents meaningful refinements.
elements.sent-2411-499: Agents optimized for predefined tests may not generalize well to evolving user needs or task complexities.
elements.sent-2411-500: To bridge this gap, evaluation must function as a continuous feedback loop, where real-world insights directly inform prompt tuning, retrieval optimization, and agent reasoning updates.
elements.sent-2411-501: Agents remain reactive rather than adaptive: Without mechanisms to systematically integrate evaluation feedback, LLM agents risk stagnation—responding to new inputs without evolving their decision-making processes.
elements.sent-2411-502: A structured approach to evaluation-driven adaptation is needed, where model-level refinements (e.g., retrieval and prompt optimization) are complemented by system-level adjustments (e.g., refining workflow coordination and external tool usage).
elements.sent-2411-503: Underutilization of failure patterns as a learning signal: Evaluations often expose recurring weaknesses—such as reasoning errors or ineffective tool use—but these insights remain disconnected from agent improvement cycles.
elements.sent-2411-504: Future evaluation strategies should shift from pass/fail assessments toward diagnostic evaluations that systematically track failure trends over time, enabling proactive mitigation rather than reactive fixes.
elements.subsubsection-2411-010: Limitations of AI-Only Evaluations and the Need for Human-in-the-Loop
elements.sent-2411-505: Our analysis (Fig.~\ref{fig:ai-human-hybrid-evaluators ) reveals a strong reliance on AI evaluators in academic studies, with 88.06\% (118/134) employing fully automated methods, minimal human involvement in 2.99\% (4/134), and only 8.96\% (12/134) using hybrid human-AI evaluations.}
elements.sent-2411-506: In contrast, grey literature presents a more balanced distribution: 44.44\% (12/27) use AI-only evaluations, another 44.44\% (12/27) integrate hybrid human-AI approaches, and 11.11\% (3/27) prioritize human-led evaluations.
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.6\linewidth]{ai_human_hybrid_evaluators_updated.pdf} \caption{Comparison of AI, Human, and Hybrid Evaluators in Academic vs. Grey Literature} \label{fig:ai-human-hybrid-evaluators}
elements.sent-2411-507: AI-only evaluators, which rely predominantly on automated scoring or computation-based assessments (e.g., LLM-based evaluators~\cite{zheng2023judging,vertex_ai_eval_metrics ), offer scalability and consistency but often lack sensitivity to qualitative dimensions such as reasoning coherence, ethical judgment, and user-centric factors.}
elements.sent-2411-508: The disparity indicates that academic evaluations typically prioritize automation efficiency, while industry practices recognize that human evaluators contribute essential interpretability, context-awareness, and ethical oversight, especially in ambiguous or high-stakes scenarios.. Implications:.
elements.sent-2411-509: Automated evaluations may amplify biases: AI evaluators inherit biases from their training data, potentially reinforcing systemic inequities if not balanced by human judgment.
elements.sent-2411-510: Industry best practices recommend hybrid evaluation, as it provides deeper insights into nuanced aspects of agent performance, such as ethical reasoning, user acceptance, and coherence~\cite{subramaniam2025genaipatterns .}
elements.sent-2411-511: Critical risks in high-stakes applications: Domains such as healthcare, finance, and legal decision-making require rigorous oversight due to the severe consequences of errors.
elements.sent-2411-512: AI-only evaluations may fail to detect subtle yet critical failure patterns, making human oversight mandatory to ensure thorough assessment and mitigate potential harm~\cite{subramaniam2025genaipatterns .}
elements.subsection-2411-016: Bridging Evaluation Challenges to Design Solutions
elements.sent-2411-513: The challenges outlined above highlight critical gaps in existing LLM agent evaluation practices, revealing limitations in lifecycle coverage, metric design, system integration, adaptability, evaluation-driven refinement, and the balance between human and AI assessors.
elements.sent-2411-514: Addressing these issues requires a structured approach that integrates evaluation not only as a validation mechanism but as a core driver of agent design and development.
elements.sent-2411-515: To systematically address these challenges, we propose a process model that embeds evaluation throughout the LLM agent lifecycle, ensuring continuous feedback loops and adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-516: Additionally, our reference architecture provides an architectural framework that operationalizes evaluation-driven design, supporting both model- and system-level refinements.
elements.sent-2411-517: Table~\ref{tab:gap_mapping summarizes how each identified challenge is mapped to key strategies within the process model and reference architecture.}
elements.sent-2411-518: This structured linkage ensures that evaluation is not merely a final assessment stage but an active and iterative component of LLM agent development.

elements.sent-2411-519: This structured approach ensures that evaluation is not a passive, post-deployment checkpoint but an active driver of LLM agent improvement.
elements.sent-2411-520: The following sections detail the proposed process model (Sec.
elements.sent-2411-521: Building on the insights from RQ1, we now introduce a structured Process Model for LLM Agent Evaluation (Fig.~\ref{fig:evalflow ).}
elements.sent-2411-522: While traditional evaluation approaches often focus on pre-deployment testing or rely on high-level success metrics, LLM agents require a lifecycle-spanning, risk-aware, and adaptive evaluation process.
elements.sent-2411-523: Our model integrates offline and online evaluation, structured feedback loops, and real-world adaptability, ensuring continuous monitoring and refinement of LLM agents across their lifecycle.
elements.sent-2411-524: By specifying evaluation activities, risk-based prioritization, and iterative improvement mechanisms, this process model bridges conceptual evaluation challenges with practical implementation needs, laying the foundation for the reference architecture in RQ3.
elements.sent-2411-525: A well-defined evaluation plan is essential for structured, goal-driven, and risk-aware evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-526: Without it, evaluations risk being reactive, misaligned with real-world constraints, and unable to support continuous improvement.
elements.sent-2411-527: Unlike traditional evaluation plans, which are static and pre-defined, our approach is dynamic, risk-aware, and lifecycle-spanning.
elements.sent-2411-528: It adapts to evolving user needs, governance regulations, and system architecture, ensuring evaluations remain relevant, structured, and actionable.
elements.sent-2411-529: This dynamic adaptation is facilitated through feedback loops, continuous monitoring, and iterative refinement processes integrated into the evaluation framework.
elements.sent-2411-530: Key Inputs: The evaluation plan is informed by three primary inputs:.
elements.sent-2411-531: User Goals: High-level objectives representing user needs (e.g., “An LLM-powered financial advisor should provide tax suggestions tailored to individual income brackets and locations”).
elements.sent-2411-532: These goals capture user expectations and operational contexts, serving as a foundation for defining evaluation objectives.
elements.sent-2411-533: Governance Requirements: Legal, ethical, and safety constraints (e.g., ensuring compliance with the EU AI Act).
elements.sent-2411-534: These requirements outline compliance criteria and ethical guidelines that the evaluation must address \cite{guldimann2024compl .}
elements.sent-2411-535: Initial Agent Architecture: The system’s structural and behavioral blueprint, detailing key components, dependencies, and initial risk trade-offs.
elements.sent-2411-536: This input provides insight into technical complexities and potential areas of vulnerability that require focused evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-537: Process Steps: The evaluation planning process involves five key activities to ensure systematic, risk-aware, and adaptable assessments:.
elements.sent-2411-538: Understand User Goals: Translates high-level goals into structured, testable evaluation scenarios, defining expected interactions, environmental conditions, and task outcomes \cite{beck2022test,bartz2020benchmarking,adzic2011specification .}
elements.sent-2411-539: For example, an LLM-driven legal assistant tasked with drafting contracts or summarizing case law must ensure factual accuracy and citation validity.
elements.sent-2411-540: Errors in legal interpretations, outdated statutes, or incorrect case references could lead to severe consequences, such as contractual disputes or legal malpractice.
elements.sent-2411-541: Incorporate Governance Requirements: Integrate legal and ethical constraints into the evaluation plan to ensure compliance with applicable regulations like the EU AI Act \cite{guldimann2024compl .}
elements.sent-2411-542: For instance, in the US, a healthcare LLM agent must comply with the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act, necessitating thorough evaluations of its data handling practices.
elements.sent-2411-543: Analyze Initial Agent Architecture: Identify potential risks, dependencies, and failure points within the system's architecture.
elements.sent-2411-544: This includes analyzing the agent's reasoning pipelines, guardrails, external integrations, and memory systems.
elements.sent-2411-545: For example, an agent accessing real-time data via APIs may require evaluations of RAG capabilities, API latency, and privacy safeguards.
elements.sent-2411-546: Risk-Based Prioritization: Classify and prioritize evaluation scenarios along three dimensions: (1) impact severity (e.g., safety or financial consequences), (2) domain sensitivity (e.g., healthcare versus general use), and (3) stakeholder risk tolerance~\cite{sarmah2024choose,eugeneyan2023llmEvals .}
elements.sent-2411-547: To address challenges in quantifying risks at the initial stages of system development, a dual analysis is recommended—employing forward analysis to explore potential scenarios and pathways that may lead to risk, alongside backward analysis to derive appropriate thresholds for early evaluation units from the expected final harm.
elements.sent-2411-548: For instance, in high-risk domains such as medical diagnosis, strict evaluation criteria would be enforced, while in lower-risk contexts like email drafting, more flexible criteria can be applied.

elements.sent-2411-639: Our design is guided by three key principles:.
elements.sent-2411-640: Lifecycle Integration: Evaluation is embedded across both pre-deployment (design and development) and post-deployment (runtime and redevelopment) stages, ensuring that insights are continuously available for iterative improvement.
elements.sent-2411-641: Meaningful Feedback Loops: Proactive feedback loops enable rapid runtime adjustments and comprehensive offline refinements, so that evaluation results actively shape system behavior.
elements.sent-2411-642: Evaluation as an Active Driver: Evaluation mechanisms are designed not merely for diagnostics but to directly inform decision-making and adaptive processes, fostering self-improvement in response to changing operational demands.
elements.sent-2411-643: Building on insights from the MLR and process model, our architecture extends previous reference architectures for designing foundation model based systems \cite{lu2024towards and responsible AI systems \cite{lu2023responsible by uniquely centering evaluation as a first-class architectural concern.}}
elements.sent-2411-644: The architecture comprises three interconnected layers that systematically integrate evaluation throughout the agent lifecycle.
elements.sent-2411-645: The Supply Chain Layer establishes the foundational design, functionality, and evaluation criteria for the LLM agent.
elements.sent-2411-646: It comprises a series of preparatory steps—represented with dotted lines to indicate their supportive role—that collectively set the stage for effective evaluation and subsequent system adaptation.. Key steps include:.
elements.sent-2411-647: Plan and Design: Define user goals, governance requirements, and a high-level architecture to shape evaluation criteria and identify relevant scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-648: This step underpins benchmark selection, test case creation, and alignment with compliance and performance standards.
elements.sent-2411-649: Collect and Process Data: Gather and preprocess data for model fine-tuning and agent testing, with an emphasis on preparing evaluation data for off-the-shelf LLMs.
elements.sent-2411-650: Build/Select and Evaluate Model: Assess proprietary models using tools such as system cards (e.g., the OpenAI o1 System Card \cite{openaio1systemcard ) or through black-box API testing, while open-source models are fine-tuned and evaluated against defined performance goals.}
elements.sent-2411-651: Build and Evaluate System: Integrate the LLM with core system components (e.g., guardrails) and conduct system-level evaluations to verify functional correctness, quality, and risk.
elements.sent-2411-652: Offline evaluations confirm agent readiness prior to deployment.
elements.sent-2411-653: A dedicated repository consolidates key artifacts—test cases, safety cases, and offline evaluation results—that serve as performance baselines for subsequent evaluation-driven refinements.
elements.sent-2411-654: The Agent Layer forms the adaptive core of the LLM agent, supporting information processing, reasoning and planning, and dynamic behavior adjustment.
elements.sent-2411-655: This layer is divided into two segments: External Environments and the Agent Module.
elements.sent-2411-656: Users: Who offer explicit or implicit feedback that guides the agent’s actions and informs iterative improvements.
elements.sent-2411-657: Other Agents: With which the agent collaborates via task distribution, data sharing, or multi-agent communication \cite{chen2023agentverse , enabling coordinated task execution.}
elements.sent-2411-658: Tools: Task-specific APIs and processing modules that the agent uses to execute workflows and enhance decision-making.
elements.sent-2411-659: Knowledge Bases: Static or dynamic sources (e.g., databases, ontologies) that provide contextual and domain-specific information to support reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-660: Context Engine: Aggregates data from external environments and memory, ensuring responses remain contextually relevant.
elements.sent-2411-661: Reasoning \& Planning: Generates actionable plans and workflows based on contextual inputs, serving as a key target for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-662: Execution: Orchestrates task sequences, exception handling, and external system interactions to ensure smooth operation.
elements.sent-2411-663: Multi-layered Guardrails: Enforce correctness and safety constraints, triggering alerts when deviations from expected behavior occur \cite{shamsujjoha2024taxonomymultilayeredruntimeguardrails .}
elements.sent-2411-664: Memory: Stores operational feedback, user preferences, and past decisions, enabling iterative adaptation over time.
elements.sent-2411-665: A key aspect of the Agent Layer is its integration with evaluation insights.
elements.sent-2411-666: Artifacts from the Supply Chain Layer—such as test cases and safety cases—serve as evaluation references, while runtime feedback loops ensure continuous adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-667: For example, in a financial risk assessment scenario, if an agent consistently misclassifies regulatory risks, evaluation insights can trigger refinements in decision policies, prompting updates to its reasoning models and knowledge retrieval mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-668: The Operation Layer serves as the central hub for continuous evaluation and adaptation, ensuring that evaluation insights directly inform agent refinements and system evolution.
elements.sent-2411-669: It consists of two key components: Evaluation and AgentOps Infrastructure.
elements.sent-2411-670: Unlike traditional architectures that treat evaluation as a periodic validation step, our design embeds continuous and adaptive evaluation mechanisms, ensuring that real-time assessment drives operational improvements.
elements.sent-2411-671: Key features include using hybrid evaluator for continuous evaluations, whose results in turn serve as direct inputs for system modifications:.
elements.sent-2411-672: Immediate runtime adaptations: Evaluation results trigger dynamic updates to agent operations, such as refining prompt structures, adjusting reasoning pathways, or modifying retrieval and tool invocation strategies based on performance signals and detected inconsistencies.
elements.sent-2411-673: Systematic long-term refinements: Evaluation findings guide structured improvements to safety cases, the evolution of evaluation datasets, and the calibration of decision-making heuristics.
elements.sent-2411-674: This ensures that the agent continuously adapts to shifting operational conditions while maintaining alignment with performance and governance requirements.
elements.sent-2411-675: Escalation to human evaluators for high-uncertainty scenarios: AI-based evaluations efficiently handle routine assessments, but when confidence thresholds are exceeded—such as detecting ambiguous failures, ethical concerns, or novel operational risks—evaluations are escalated for human review to ensure nuanced, context-aware judgments.
elements.sent-2411-676: Continuous test and safety case evolution: Rather than relying on static benchmarks, evaluation insights dynamically expand assessment coverage by generating new test cases and safety constraints.
elements.sent-2411-677: This adaptive approach ensures that emerging risks, edge cases, and real-world operational shifts are progressively integrated into the evaluation framework.
elements.sent-2411-678: The AgentOps Infrastructure ensures seamless integration of evaluation-driven insights into operational workflows, providing essential monitoring and intervention mechanisms:.
elements.sent-2411-679: Proactive Issue Detection: Observability mechanisms track anomalies and deviations, triggering automated evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-680: Feedback Integration Pipeline: Links evaluation outputs (e.g., production logs) directly to refinement processes, ensuring real-time and iterative improvement.
elements.sent-2411-681: In summary, this architecture establishes evaluation as an integral mechanism for continuous agent adaptation and learning, ensuring LLM agents remain safe, context-aware, and aligned with evolving operational requirements.
elements.sent-2411-682: By bridging gaps in lifecycle-spanning evaluation, real-time adaptation, and evaluation-driven agent development, it enables a structured approach to developing more safer, and more performant LLM agents.
elements.sent-2411-683: This section validates and evaluates the three proposed contributions, starting with a methodological evaluation of the MLR results that form the foundation for the process model and reference architecture.
elements.sent-2411-684: The evaluation includes both theoretical validation and practical application in the context of real-world scenarios, highlighting how the proposed solutions address key challenges in LLM agent evaluation.
elements.subsection-2411-017: Methodological Evaluation of the MLR Results: Threats to Validity
elements.sent-2411-685: Internal Validity: Potential biases in data selection and synthesis may impact the accuracy of the findings.
elements.sent-2411-686: To address this, we implemented a rigorous MLR process with well-defined inclusion and exclusion criteria.
elements.sent-2411-687: The screening process was enhanced by internal reviews to ensure consistency and minimize subjective biases.
elements.sent-2411-688: Practitioner discussions further validated the synthesized insights, providing practical alignment with real-world evaluation practices.
elements.sent-2411-689: Any discrepancies during synthesis were resolved through iterative discussions to maintain methodological rigor and alignment.
elements.sent-2411-690: External Validity: While the MLR is grounded in a comprehensive synthesis of academic and industry sources, its applicability across diverse domains and operational contexts has not been extensively validated.
elements.sent-2411-691: Future work will focus on real-world case studies and scalability assessments to evaluate its adaptability, ensuring that domain-specific nuances are addressed.
elements.sent-2411-692: These real-world case studies will focus on application domains such as healthcare and finance to ensure that the findings are both transferable and scalable across contexts.
elements.sent-2411-693: Construct Validity: The unification of diverse evaluation practices into a single framework risks oversimplification or misrepresentation.
elements.sent-2411-694: To mitigate this, the data extraction and synthesis process was explicitly documented, ensuring transparency and reproducibility.
elements.sent-2411-695: This documentation allows for critical review and facilitates refinement in future iterations of the framework.
elements.sent-2411-696: Additionally, by continuously updating and refining the evaluation practices based on ongoing research, we ensure that the framework remains relevant and accurate.
elements.sent-2411-697: The process model provides a structured framework for integrating evaluation across the lifecycle of LLM agents.
elements.sent-2411-698: To validate its applicability, adaptability, and effectiveness, we employed a two-pronged approach: (1) a real-world case study involving the design and development of a LLM-based Tax Assistant system in Australia, and (2) a synthesis of industry practices based on publicly available practitioner reports and posts.
elements.subsubsection-2411-011: Case Study: Validation with an LLM-based Tax Assistant
elements.sent-2411-699: To validate the proposed process model, we applied it to a real-world project involving the design and development of an LLM-based Tax Assistant.
elements.sent-2411-700: This system is designed to assist tax professionals in Australia by providing automated analysis of complex tax scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-701: It employs RAG to retrieve and integrate knowledge from Australian Taxation Office (ATO) documentation, ensuring that its responses are both informed and contextually relevant.
elements.sent-2411-703: The evaluation commenced with the development of a comprehensive plan tailored to the Tax Assistant’s core function: providing professional, accurate, and compliant tax recommendations to tax professionals operating under Australian tax laws.
elements.sent-2411-704: User goals focused on supporting tax professionals by analyzing tax scenarios and generating reliable advice based on ATO guidelines.
elements.sent-2411-705: Governance requirements emphasized strict compliance with ATO regulations, enforcing robust data privacy and security measures in alignment with the Australian Privacy Principles (APPs), and adhering to Australia's AI ethics principles to foster trust and transparency in AI-assisted tax advisory.
elements.sent-2411-706: The agent’s architecture was analyzed using the AgentArcEval framework~\cite{lu2024evaluating to identify critical components and risk factors.}
elements.sent-2411-707: A risk-based prioritization strategy was implemented to assess potential failure points and legal risks.
elements.sent-2411-708: For example, a user might inadvertently submit personal financial data for tax calculation, or the system could generate responses containing sensitive information beyond the scope of retrieval.
elements.sent-2411-709: Hallucinated or misleading tax advice could lead to serious legal and financial repercussions.
elements.sent-2411-710: To mitigate these risks, the evaluation plan outlined key objectives such as retrieval accuracy, generation quality, response efficiency, and data privacy protection, each categorized with priority levels based on their potential impact.
elements.sent-2411-712: For evaluation, DeepEval was selected as the primary framework, as it provides a comprehensive evaluation suite supporting various LLM assessment metrics, including faithfulness, answer relevancy, contextual relevancy, contextual precision, hallucination, toxicity, and bias.
elements.sent-2411-713: Due to the open-ended nature of tax advisory questions, special emphasis was placed on contextual and answer relevancy.
elements.sent-2411-714: Unlike tax compliance questions, which often have definitive yes/no answers, tax advisory questions involve multiple valid interpretations depending on the user’s risk tolerance and tax strategy.
elements.sent-2411-715: After an initial benchmarking evaluation, additional tax-specific datasets were curated.
elements.sent-2411-716: This process involved collaboration with tax professionals and ATO representatives to ensure real-world applicability.
elements.sent-2411-717: The test cases covered a broad range of scenarios, including standard tax inquiries, complex tax deductions, and cross-border taxation issues.
elements.sent-2411-718: Unlike general-purpose LLM applications, the availability of real-world tax data was sufficient for evaluation purposes.
elements.sent-2411-719: Consequently, while DeepEval supports synthetic data generation, it was not required in this case.
elements.sent-2411-720: Step 3: Conduct Offline and Online Evaluations.
elements.sent-2411-721: Since our involvement in the project was primarily pre-deployment, the evaluation focused on offline assessments, targeting both intermediate retrieval processes and final generated tax advice.
elements.sent-2411-722: Given that a proprietary LLM API was employed, our evaluation focused on the end-to-end performance of the Tax Assistant rather than assessing the underlying LLM model itself.
elements.sent-2411-723: Evaluations were structured to be modular, allowing intermediate and final evaluations to be conducted independently based on specific objectives.
elements.sent-2411-724: Although full-scale online deployment and post-deployment evaluations were beyond our project scope, we conducted pilot testing within controlled simulated environments using DeepEval’s LLM observability tools.
elements.sent-2411-725: These tests enabled detailed logging and tracing, allowing for quick identification of performance bottlenecks and retrieval errors.
elements.sent-2411-726: Additionally, the system incorporated human-in-the-loop feedback, ensuring that flagged responses could be reviewed and refined based on expert input.
elements.sent-2411-728: Evaluation results were leveraged to drive both runtime adaptations and offline redevelopment improvements.
elements.sent-2411-729: Real-time feedback mechanisms enabled dynamic adjustments to retrieval workflows and reasoning/planning processes.
elements.sent-2411-730: Specific issues, such as redundant retrievals or irrelevant citations, were identified and addressed through workflow optimizations.
elements.sent-2411-731: The agent architecture was refined to improve RAG accuracy and enhance safety mechanisms, particularly by adjusting retrieval guardrails to prevent leakage of sensitive or outdated tax information.
elements.sent-2411-732: Since a proprietary LLM API was used, modifications to the model itself were not within our scope.
elements.sent-2411-733: Instead, improvements focused on optimizing system-level performance and enhancing retrieval efficiency.
elements.subsubsection-2411-012: Insights from Practitioner Reports and Posts
elements.sent-2411-734: To validate the proposed process model, we analyzed practitioner and industry posts \cite{hamel2024llmjudge, huang2024llmmetrics, cognition2024review .}
elements.sent-2411-735: These sources detail real-world evaluation practices for LLM systems/agents and illustrate how structured evaluation frameworks are applied in practice.
elements.sent-2411-736: Practitioners consistently emphasize the importance of starting with interpretable evaluation frameworks.
elements.sent-2411-737: For example, the Cognition.AI post \cite{cognition2024review describes using binary pass/fail metrics combined with detailed critiques to assess outcomes, ensuring that evaluators explicitly confirm whether outputs meet defined criteria and uncover hidden assumptions about system behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-738: This approach directly supports Step 1 of our process model, where the evaluation plan is established by defining clear objectives, criteria, and risk prioritizations.
elements.sent-2411-739: In practice, pass/fail metrics help verify that an LLM agent avoids unsafe outputs in high-stakes scenarios, while accompanying critiques pinpoint areas for refinement—such as managing ambiguous inputs.
elements.sent-2411-740: Furthermore, insights from the Microsoft post \cite{huang2024llmmetrics and the Cognition AI evaluation methodology underscore the challenges of dynamic, open-ended tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-741: They advocate for benchmarks that evolve over time, drawing on real-world data (including production logs and user feedback) to capture data saturation and shifting requirements.
elements.sent-2411-742: This dynamic benchmarking approach validates Step 2 of our process model, where test case development integrates both scenario-specific and synthetic data to adapt to emerging challenges.
elements.sent-2411-743: For instance, dynamic benchmarks might incorporate user feedback to refine test cases for conversational adaptability or task-specific accuracy, ensuring ongoing relevance across diverse contexts.
elements.sent-2411-744: The Cognition AI post \cite{cognition2024review also highlights the importance of conducting both offline and online evaluations—a central tenet of our Step 3.}
elements.sent-2411-745: Offline evaluations use controlled environments and synthetic data to establish performance baselines, while online evaluations rely on simulated user interactions, operational metrics (such as latency, resource utilization, and coherence), and autonomous feedback mechanisms.

elements.sent-2411-754: This comparative analysis examines the differences and improvements introduced in our architecture, demonstrating its novel contributions to evaluation-driven LLM agent development.
elements.sent-2411-755: The comparative analysis is structured around several key dimensions below.
elements.sent-2411-756: The Evaluation-Driven Reference Architecture advances beyond existing designs by embedding evaluation as a core architectural principle rather than a post-hoc or auxiliary process (see Table~\ref{tab:comparative_analysis ).}
elements.sent-2411-757: Architectural Scope: The primary focus of the architecture (e.g., general LLM agent design vs. evaluation-driven design).
elements.sent-2411-758: Modularity and Extensibility: The extent to which architectural components are modular and adaptable to evolving system requirements, particularly for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-759: Support for Evaluation-Driven Development: The degree to which the architecture explicitly integrates evaluation as a first-class concern across the LLM lifecycle.
elements.sent-2411-760: Observability and Monitoring: The presence of structured observability features such as logging, performance tracking, failure analysis, and real-time monitoring for continuous evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-761: Human-in-the-Loop Integration: The extent to which the architecture enables human oversight, feedback loops, and iterative adjustments to improve model performance and reliability.

elements.sent-2411-764: Performance Efficiency: The AgentOps Infrastructure supports real-time monitoring and adaptive feedback loops, minimizing latency in detecting and responding to performance issues.

elements.sent-2411-766: Compatibility: By explicitly integrating external environments—including users, tools, knowledge bases, and other agents—the architecture facilitates seamless compatibility and interoperability, ensuring coherent interactions within complex operational ecosystems.

elements.sent-2411-778: Overall, the analytical validation confirms that the proposed architecture comprehensively addresses ISO/IEC 25010 quality attributes, providing a robust and adaptive foundation for evaluation-driven development of LLM agents.
elements.sent-2411-779: This paper introduced a structured approach for integrating evaluation systematically into the development and operation of LLM agents.
elements.sent-2411-780: By addressing three core research questions, we advanced understanding in the evaluation-driven development of complex, adaptive LLM agents: First, through a comprehensive Multivocal Literature Review (RQ1), we identified critical gaps in current evaluation practices, including fragmented lifecycle coverage, over-reliance on aggregated metrics, insufficient adaptive evaluations, and inadequate integration of human evaluators.
elements.sent-2411-781: These findings underscored the necessity for lifecycle-spanning, context-sensitive evaluation methods capable of continuous and dynamic assessment.
elements.sent-2411-782: Second, we proposed a structured process model (RQ2) to procedurally embed continuous evaluation into the development lifecycle of LLM agents.
elements.sent-2411-783: This model integrates risk-based prioritization, continuous feedback loops, and adaptive testing strategies, ensuring that evaluation outcomes actively guide both immediate runtime adjustments and long-term redevelopment activities.
elements.sent-2411-784: Third, we developed a reference architecture (RQ3) that architecturally supports this evaluation-driven approach.
elements.sent-2411-785: This architecture embeds evaluation mechanisms at multiple levels—including supply chain preparations, adaptive agent operations, and continuous operational monitoring—facilitating real-time adaptations and proactive refinements informed by both AI and human evaluator inputs.
elements.sent-2411-786: Collectively, these contributions establish a cohesive framework where evaluation transcends its traditional diagnostic role, becoming an integral, proactive driver of agent evolution.. \bibliographystyle{unsrt.}
